label: NetEase Cloud Music API
summary: NetEase Cloud Music API wrappers are a strong SongMetadata candidate for Asian catalog enrichment because they expose search, song detail, album, artist, playlist, and NetEase provider IDs.
The best current upstream is NeteaseCloudMusicApiEnhanced, a maintained MIT revival of the original large Node API wrapper after the original Binaryify GitHub repository was archived.
This row should be used as a provider-specific metadata authority candidate, especially when NetEase SongSense returns only IDs or partial metadata that need enrichment.

signals: NeteaseCloudMusicApiEnhanced is active, MIT-licensed, and describes itself as a broad NetEase Cloud Music Node.js API service.
The original Binaryify GitHub repository is archived, but points readers to the package and external continuation context.
simple-netease-cloud-music documents search, playlist, picture, artist, album, lyric, URL, and song-detail calls with a lightweight Node API.
The Go and Rust wrapper evidence shows that NetEase metadata access is not limited to one JavaScript package, but Node remains the best initial Axolync fit.

notes: Most relevant wrappers are permissive OSS, but the metadata itself comes from NetEase-hosted services without an Axolync-owned formal API contract.

entries.subject: NeteaseCloudMusicApiEnhanced api-enhanced
notes: Primary recommended wrapper because it is maintained and broad.

entries.subject: simple-netease-cloud-music
notes: Lightweight but archived; useful as fallback design evidence.

entries.subject: chaunsin netease-cloud-music
notes: Go API and CLI evidence; more suitable for a host service than browser-local code.

entries.subject: NetEase Cloud Music metadata service
notes: Provider terms and endpoint stability remain external risks.
API Enhanced is broad and maintained, but it is still a reverse-engineered wrapper rather than an official partner contract.
SongMetadata normalization should keep NetEase IDs, aliases, translated names, album art, duration, and provider provenance explicit.
A host-owned integration avoids browser CORS and lets Axolync centralize search normalization, caching, and rate-limit handling.

methods.notes: JS wrappers exist, but direct browser use may hit CORS and provider request-shape constraints.
This is metadata HTTP/API work, not an engine to port.
A native bridge can proxy calls, but a shared host is usually simpler for metadata.
An extension can work around browser constraints, but metadata lookup does not inherently need extension-level privileges.
Userscript access would be fragile and not a good long-term metadata authority.
A desktop helper could own a local Node or Go wrapper, but deployment overhead is higher than a shared host.
Python could wrap the HTTP API, but the strongest maintained upstream is Node.
Best first lane because the maintained API Enhanced wrapper is Node-oriented and broad.
CLI wrapping is possible around Go/Node tools, but less clean for live metadata enrichment.
A Rust or Go host is possible, but there is no clear advantage over Node for the first adapter.
Endpoint calls are JS-adjacent, but a local rewrite would inherit service drift and browser constraints.
A shared service can cache search/detail responses and isolate NetEase endpoint drift.
A per-request service is plausible for metadata normalization, especially around search and detail lookups.
No official platform SDK route was found for NetEase metadata.

notes: Use NeteaseCloudMusicApiEnhanced as the primary proof source.
Keep the SongMetadata row focused on provider ID enrichment and normalized metadata, not playback or download behavior.

openQuestions: Should NetEase SongMetadata be an observer-style enrichment adapter or only run when NetEase provider IDs are already present?
Which NetEase fields should be normalized into the shared SongMetadata contract versus preserved as provider-specific extras?
